topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat on my worn welcome mat, an unwelcome intrusion in the quiet hallway. It was addressed to 'Apartment 3B,' the unit I had vacated three weeks prior. A knot tightened in my chest as I picked it up. The tape was old, slightly brittle, and the handwriting on the label was unfamiliar, looping and hurried. My fingers traced the faded ink, a cold prickle running up my arm. Who would think to send something to an empty space? I set the box down on the dusty floor, my breath catching in my throat. Every instinct screamed at me to leave it, to ignore the mystery, but I couldn't seem to move. I walked to the door, my hand hovering over the knob, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I stared at the package as if it held a secret map leading somewhere I didn't want to go. It arrived with no note, just a heavy, plain brown box. The weight of it felt disproportionate to its size, pressing down on my palms. I paced the small living room, the floorboards groaning under my restless steps. My gaze kept darting toward the door, half-expecting someone to materialize, to explain the presence of this unsolicited item. A low hum of unease vibrated beneath my skin. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at strands that felt suddenly too heavy, and I found myself compulsively checking the peephole, my knuckles white against the cold metal.

[story 3]

It was tucked beneath the door, a silent accusation wrapped in brown paper. I hadn't expected anything. The thought surfaced unbidden, a sharp, unwelcome intrusion into my day. When I finally retrieved it, the paper felt strangely slick. I carried it to the kitchen table, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead seeming too loud. I didn't open it immediately. Instead, I circled the table twice, my gaze fixed on the contents—a small, sealed envelope. A strange tension coiled in my shoulders, making them ache. I picked up the envelope, my jaw clenching so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars, and I knew, with a certainty that chilled me to the bone, that opening it would change everything.
